does: This paper investigates vulnerabilities in PPO-aligned language models by studying a controlled setting where GPT-2 is fine-tuned via PPO to generate positive sentiment responses using a static sentiment classifier as the reward function. The authors apply mechanistic interpretability techniques to examine model internals before and after alignment, then conduct two main experiments: attempting to directly manipulate the model to produce negative sentiment outputs despite alignment, and modifying the reward function to penalize identified harmful weights. The work exposes concrete failure modes of PPO alignment in a simplified but interpretable setting where model weights and activations are visible to end-users.
connects: This work extends recent research on jailbreaking and vulnerabilities in aligned language models by grounding the analysis in mechanistic interpretability rather than treating alignment as a black box. It builds on prior work studying how RLHF and related alignment methods can be circumvented or exploited, while also connecting to the growing literature on interpreting what alignment procedures actually change in model internals. The paper responds to the practical concern that sophisticated reward functions in real-world alignment may have exploitable gaps, using positive sentiment as a tractable testbed inspired by actual alignment scenarios but with full observability of model components.
enables: This research points toward more robust alignment approaches that account for interpretability-informed vulnerabilities. It suggests that future alignment methods should incorporate interpretability insights to identify and patch specific weight-based exploits rather than relying solely on reward function design. The work enables subsequent research on mechanistic alignment—directly modifying interpretable model components rather than optimizing through RL—and motivates investigation of whether similar vulnerabilities exist in larger, more complex aligned models where interpretability is harder. It also raises important questions about the feasibility of alignment when end-users have white-box access to model internals.
